original_abstract: Abstract Background The global burden of invasive fungal infections (IFIs) has shown an upsurge in recent years due to the higher load of immunocompromised patients suffering from various diseases. The role of early and accurate diagnosis in the aggressive containment of the fungal infection at the initial stages becomes crucial thus, preventing the development of a life-threatening situation. With the changing demands of clinical mycology, the field of fungal diagnostics has evolved and come a long way from traditional methods of microscopy and culturing to more advanced non-culture-based tools. With the advent of more powerful approaches such as novel PCR assays, T2 Candida, microfluidic chip technology, next generation sequencing, new generation biosensors, nanotechnology-based tools, artificial intelligence-based models, the face of fungal diagnostics is constantly changing for the better. All these advances have been reviewed here giving the latest update to our readers in the most orderly flow. Main text A detailed literature survey was conducted by the team followed by data collection, pertinent data extraction, in-depth analysis, and composing the various sub-sections and the final review. The review is unique in its kind as it discusses the advances in molecular methods; advances in serology-based methods; advances in biosensor technology; and advances in machine learning-based models, all under one roof. To the best of our knowledge, there has been no review covering all of these fields (especially biosensor technology and machine learning using artificial intelligence) with relevance to invasive fungal infections. Conclusion The review will undoubtedly assist in updating the scientific community’s understanding of the most recent advancements that are on the horizon and that may be implemented as adjuncts to the traditional diagnostic algorithms.
humanized_abstract: Abstract Background: Incidence of IFIs has increased all over the globe in recent years mainly because of the increasing incidence of immunocompromised conditions. So the diagnosis at the initial stages is crucial and for this the diagnostic process has to be done in a most effective and rapid manner. It has to be remembered that time is of essence when it comes to management of a IFI. Moreover the advancements in the field of clinical mycology have made way for many rapid and more reliable and sensitive detection methods and have changed the face of clinical mycology in such a way that the classical and more traditional methods such as the use of microscope and culture medium etc are considered to be old-fashioned. Also the molecular techniques like PCR and other associated techniques like genotypic tests, probe-based hybridization system like T2 Candida, microfluidic chip-based system and the next generation sequencing, biosensors of new generation along with nanotechnology are making the fungal diagnosis a easy and simpler task with very good accuracy and with less time consumption. Here we have put up the progress in all these latest diagnostic technologies in a most ordered manner. Main text: The whole information required for this review was done through an extensive literature survey carried out by the group of researchers including the collection and extraction of data; detailed information analysis of all the related literature; compiling of various information contained in all the sub-sections required to prepare this comprehensive review. This review paper highlights the latest progresses and advances in molecular methods as well as in the conventional methods such as serology-based methods with recent advancements and also about the advancements in the biosensor technology along with the artificial intelligence-based models which were not elaborated or reviewed at least till now in the context of IFIs and hence this paper in an endeavour to do so to the best of our knowledge. Conclusion: This review will definitely aid the scientific community to update their knowledge regarding various latest advancements which could be used as supplementary tools in laboratory diagnostic processes to diagnose an IFI at an early stage in an immunocompromised patient.
